did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and transfer learning architectures (DenseNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, InceptionResNetV2, and MobileNet) combined with various optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSprop, etc.) to classify melanoma from skin lesion images. The study investigates the impact of these optimizers on the detection performance for melanoma, a dermatology task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study investigating the impact of optimizer functions on melanoma detection using transfer learning architectures. It details specific methods (DenseNet, InceptionV3, etc., with SGD, Adam, etc.), datasets (Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset), and quantitative results (e.g., test accuracy of 0.949), which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Impact of optimizers functions on detection of Melanoma using transfer learning architectures
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of various optimizer functions on the performance of deep learning architectures for melanoma detection from skin lesion images. The researchers utilized the Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset, comprising 10,605 RGB images, to evaluate five transfer learning models: DenseNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, InceptionResNetV2, and MobileNet. These models were trained using seven different optimizers, including SGD, Adam, RMSprop, AdaDelta, AdaGrad, Adamax, and Nadam. The evaluation focused on classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and F-score to determine the most effective combination for benign versus malignant classification. The results demonstrated that the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer provided superior convergence and stability compared to other methods. Specifically, the combination of the DenseNet architecture with the SGD optimizer achieved the highest performance, yielding a test accuracy of 0.949, a sensitivity of 0.9403, and an F-score of 0.9492.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection and classification of melanoma from skin lesion images. The dataset used is the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset' (referenced via Kaggle link to HAM10000/Skin Cancer MNIST), and the classification task is described as distinguishing between benign and malignant cases, with the primary focus and named entity being melanoma.

The classification task involves distinguishing melanoma (malignant) from benign lesions. The abstract mentions 'benign versus malignant classification' and the introduction references classifying melanoma as 'benign or malignant'. Specific benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, BCC) are not explicitly listed as separate target classes in the experimental setup, but 'benign' is the comparative class to melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The paper investigates the impact of optimizer functions on the classification performance of deep learning architectures (DenseNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, InceptionResNetV2, MobileNet) for detecting melanoma from skin lesion images. The task is explicitly described as 'classification of melanoma' and 'benign versus malignant classification'. Metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, and F-score, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset (Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin lesion images used for classifying benign versus malignant melanoma. The dataset is available via Kaggle (referenced as Codella et al. [16]).

use.training: 80% of the dataset (8484 images) was used for training the deep learning models.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (2121 images) was used for testing the models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and compare the distribution, variability, and stability of validation loss values across different optimizers for each deep learning architecture.
To quantify the variability of average validation loss values for different optimizers applied to the Melanoma dataset.
To compare the classification performance (accuracy, sensitivity, F-score, loss) of different optimizer functions across various transfer learning architectures.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA). Performance comparisons between optimizers were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, F-score, loss) and visual analysis of box plots for validation loss variability.

conclusions: The Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer provides superior and more stable performance in terms of convergence rate, training speed, and classification performance compared to other optimizers (Adam, RMSprop, AdaDelta, AdaGrad, Adamax, Nadam) for melanoma detection.
The addition of a momentum parameter to the SGD optimizer reduces oscillation and training time relative to other optimizer functions.

The combination of the DenseNet architecture with the SGD optimizer achieved the highest detection performance, yielding a test accuracy of 0.949, test sensitivity of 0.9403, and test F-score of 0.9492.
SGD was consistently the most effective optimizer across all tested transfer learning architectures (DenseNet, InceptionV3, ResNet50, InceptionResNetV2, and MobileNet).
AdaGrad was identified as the optimizer producing results closest to SGD, particularly on the ResNet50 architecture, likely due to structural similarities.
The study demonstrates that optimizing the choice of optimizer function is a critical factor in maximizing the performance of deep learning models for melanoma classification.

The findings suggest that the impact of optimizer methods on deep learning success in melanoma classification is a significant area of optimization that can contribute to early detection and treatment processes.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (HAM10000/Skin Cancer MNIST) with a fixed 80/20 train-test split, lacking external validation on independent cohorts which limits generalizability.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance (5500 benign vs. 5105 malignant), which may bias performance metrics despite the reported high accuracy.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; conclusions about optimizer superiority are based on descriptive statistics and visual box plot analysis.
The study focuses exclusively on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) rather than multi-class differentiation of specific melanoma subtypes or benign lesions.
Hyperparameters such as learning rate, momentum, and weight decay were not explicitly detailed for the optimizers, limiting reproducibility of the specific SGD configuration.
The claim that this is the 'first' study to investigate optimizer impact is a novelty claim that may be contested given the extensive prior literature on deep learning for melanoma.
Results are derived from a single simulation run per configuration (50 epochs), without reporting variance across multiple random seeds or cross-validation folds.
